title: Why the Next Generation of Wi-Fi Isn’t Prioritizing Faster Speeds
gist: Wi-Fi 8 (IEEE 802.11bn), expected to certify around 2028, keeps the same 46 Gbps theoretical max speed as Wi-Fi 7 but shifts focus to reliability and lower latency via features like seamless roaming and multi-access-point coordination; chipmakers and TP-Link are already shipping early hardware ahead of certification.

themes.theme: Wi-Fi standard shifts from throughput to reliability

insights.claim: Wi-Fi 8 (IEEE 802.11bn) will keep the same theoretical maximum speed of 46 Gbps as Wi-Fi 7 and the same three frequency bands and 320-MHz max channel width, focusing instead on Ultra High Reliability features such as Multi-Access Point Coordination, Seamless Roaming Domain, and Low Latency Indication.
insights.speaker: Simon Hill
insights.so_what: Router and chipset vendors will need to compete on reliability/latency engineering rather than raw throughput marketing for the next standard cycle, changing the R&D and marketing calculus for networking hardware makers.
insights.quote: With the same theoretical maximum speed of 46 Gbps and Wi-Fi on the same three bands (2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz) with a maximum 320-MHz channel width, Wi-Fi 8 may not feel like a substantial improvement over Wi-Fi 7 for most folks.
insights.locator: How Does Wi-Fi 8 Compare to Wi-Fi 7? section

insights.claim: Wi-Fi 8 certification is expected around 2028, roughly four years after Wi-Fi 7's January 2024 certification, following the historical four-to-five-year rollout cadence for new Wi-Fi generations.
insights.speaker: Simon Hill
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete timeline anchor for when mainstream device and router refresh cycles tied to Wi-Fi 8 compliance will begin, useful for forecasting networking-hardware replacement demand.
insights.quote: Since the Wi-Fi Alliance certification for Wi-Fi 7 was officially released in January 2024, we can reasonably expect Wi-Fi 8 certification some time in 2028.

insights.claim: Despite certification not landing until 2028, chipmakers are already producing Wi-Fi 8 chipsets and TP-Link has announced Wi-Fi 8 routers and mesh systems slated for release before the end of 2026, using the IEEE's working draft as a basis.
insights.speaker: Simon Hill
insights.so_what: Early pre-certification hardware releases (as happened with Wi-Fi 7) mean networking vendors like TP-Link will command early-adopter premium pricing well before the standard is finalized, similar to the prior generation's pattern.
insights.quote: chip makers are already producing Wi-Fi 8 chipsets, and router manufacturers like TP-Link have already announced Wi-Fi 8 routers and mesh systems, with the first release slated for before the end of 2026.

insights.claim: The author advises against early Wi-Fi 8 adoption, recommending consumers wait for official certification and further price declines, since early-adopter hardware historically carries a premium and Wi-Fi 8's benefits over Wi-Fi 7 are less compelling than the Wi-Fi 6-to-7 jump was.
insights.speaker: Simon Hill
insights.so_what: Signals a slower expected replacement cycle for Wi-Fi 8 hardware than prior generations, which could temper near-term revenue upside for router/chipset makers banking on rapid upgrade demand.
insights.quote: Early adopters of Wi-Fi 8 systems can expect to pay a premium, as always, and the benefits probably won't be as compelling as the jump from Wi-Fi 6 to Wi-Fi 7. That's why I recommend to wait for the official certification and maybe even longer for prices to come down.

insights.claim: US consumers face an added complication from the FCC's foreign-made router ban, which is likely to limit available Wi-Fi 8 hardware options.
insights.speaker: Simon Hill
insights.so_what: A US regulatory restriction on foreign-made routers is a supply-side constraint that could benefit domestically-based or US-compliant networking vendors while limiting consumer choice and potentially raising prices during the Wi-Fi 8 transition.
insights.quote: For anyone in the US, the FCC's foreign-made router ban is another complication that's likely to limit your options.
